explanation_md: Construction order: `outer` creates `b(2)` first, then `inner` creates `a(1)`. When the throw unwinds the stack, destructors run in reverse order: `a` (innermost frame) is destroyed first, then `b`. Only then does the catch run.

prompt_md: What happens to local variables in a function that does NOT have a catch block, when an exception propagates through it?

options.a: Their destructors are NOT called — the memory is simply abandoned
options.b: Their destructors ARE called as the stack unwinds through that frame
options.d: The function catches the exception automatically and re-throws it

explanation_md: Stack unwinding guarantees that all local objects with destructors are destroyed as each frame is popped, regardless of whether that frame has a catch clause. This is the foundation of RAII: resources are released even during exception propagation.

options.a: The compiler rejects destructors that contain `throw` statements
options.b: If a destructor throws while the stack is already unwinding, `std::terminate` is called
options.c: Throwing in a destructor silently swallows the original exception
options.d: Exceptions from destructors are always caught by the enclosing try block

explanation_md: During stack unwinding, if a destructor throws a new exception before the first exception is caught, C++ cannot handle two simultaneous exceptions and calls `std::terminate`, aborting the program. This is why destructors are implicitly `noexcept` in C++11 and later.

`"C"` prints before `thrower()` is called. Inside `thrower()`, `"A"` prints, then the throw fires. `"B"` and `"D"` are unreachable. The catch runs printing `"E"`. Execution continues after the try/catch, printing `"F"`.
